intro:
Gilead (Hebrew Gil'ad, traditionally "heap of testimony") was the mountainous, well-watered region east of the Jordan, stretching roughly from the Yarmuk in the north to the highlands above the Arnon in the south — fertile country of forests, rich pasture, and aromatic trees, corresponding to much of modern northwestern Jordan. Renowned for its flocks and its healing balm, Gilead figures repeatedly in Israel's story as a place of refuge, conflict, and covenant.

The name first appears at the parting of Jacob and Laban, who there raised a heap of stones as a witness to their treaty and called the place "Galeed," the heap of witness (Genesis 31:48). At the conquest, the cattle-rich tribes of Reuben and Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh asked for and received this land east of the Jordan because it was good for livestock (Numbers 32:1–5); Ramoth-gilead became one of the cities of refuge (Deuteronomy 4:43). Its commercial fame is caught in the caravan of Ishmaelites "coming from Gilead with their camels bearing gum, balm, and myrrh" to whom Joseph was sold (Genesis 37:25).

Gilead is the stage for many vivid episodes. The judge Jephthah, a Gileadite, delivered Israel from Ammon (Judges 11:1); after the slaughter at the fords of the Jordan, the Gileadites exposed fugitives by the test-word "Shibboleth" (Judges 12:5–6). The prophet Elijah the Tishbite was "of the inhabitants of Gilead" (1 Kings 17:1). David fled across it to Mahanaim during Absalom's revolt (2 Samuel 17:24), and Ramoth-gilead was the perennial flashpoint of war with Aram, where Ahab fell in battle (1 Kings 22:29–37). Because it bordered the hostile east, Gilead was among the first regions stripped away when Tiglath-pileser of Assyria carried its people into exile (2 Kings 15:29).

Gilead's most enduring legacy is its balm — a fragrant medicinal resin that became a byword for healing. Jeremiah's haunting question, "Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there? Why then has the health of the daughter of my people not been restored?" (Jeremiah 8:22), laments that the remedy at hand cannot cure the nation's deeper sin-sickness — and by contrast has come, in Christian devotion, to point to the true healing found only in Christ. Amos, too, indicts Damascus for having "threshed Gilead with threshing sledges of iron" (Amos 1:3). Thus Gilead stands in Scripture as a land of testimony and refuge, of balm and of battle, witnessing both to human frailty and to the hope of healing.
hitchcock_meaning: the heap or mass of testimony

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Ramoth-gilead
City of refuge and war-flashpoint with Aram where Ahab fell (1 Kgs 22:29)
Mahanaim
Where Jacob met the angels and David sheltered from Absalom (2 Sam 17:24)
Jabesh-gilead
Town Saul rescued, which later honored his bones (1 Sam 11:1)
The Jabbok
River where Jacob wrestled with God and was named Israel (Gen 32:22–28)
Succoth
Where Jacob built booths on his return to the land (Gen 33:17)
Mizpah of Gilead
Where Jephthah made his vow before battling Ammon (Judg 11:11)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and the event below appear on the map above.

cells: The Exile of Gilead
Tiglath-pileser of Assyria carried the Transjordan tribes away captive (2 Kgs 15:29)
Jacob
Made the covenant-heap with Laban and wrestled at the Jabbok (Gen 31:48)
Laban
Pursued Jacob and made the treaty at Galeed (Gen 31:51–52)
Jephthah
Gileadite judge who delivered Israel from Ammon (Judg 11:1)
Jair
Gileadite judge with thirty sons in thirty towns (Judg 10:3–4)
Elijah
The Tishbite prophet from the inhabitants of Gilead (1 Kgs 17:1)
Ahab
King of Israel slain in battle at Ramoth-gilead (1 Kgs 22:34–37)
David
Fled across Gilead to Mahanaim during Absalom’s revolt (2 Sam 17:24)
Absalom
Pursued David into Gilead and fell in its forest (2 Sam 18:6–9)
Barzillai
Gileadite who provisioned David at Mahanaim (2 Sam 17:27–29)
